headline: Nussmeier's poise carries LSU past Michigan in defensive stalemate
body: LSU's Garrett Nussmeier overcame an ugly first half to deliver when it mattered most, steering the Tigers to a 30-24 victory over Michigan in a game that turned on field position and composure rather than explosive plays. Nussmeier completed 22 of 38 passes for 275 yards and two touchdowns, never forcing a dangerous decision despite Michigan's defense keeping him off schedule throughout the afternoon.

The real battle was fought in the trenches. Michigan outgained LSU by 14 total yards and held a clear advantage in rushing production—Wendtland's 99 yards on nine carries gave the Wolverines a physical edge up front—yet those advantages never translated into sustained scoring drives. LSU's defense, anchored by Mangum's seven tackles, forced Michigan into uncomfortable third-and-long situations repeatedly, while the Tigers' rushing attack, though modest at 178 yards, moved the chains when LSU needed six.

Hasty's 79-yard receiving performance proved the difference in tight moments. The wideout's ability to create separation gave Nussmeier a consistent outlet, and his 79 yards effectively neutralized Michigan's secondary depth issues. Gabriel managed only 203 yards for Michigan despite matching Nussmeier in completions, a sign the Tigers' pass rush periodically disrupted timing and forced hurried releases that limited the Wolverines' vertical game.

Neither offense was flawless. The absence of turnovers from both teams underscored how carefully each side operated, but LSU's slight edge in explosive plays—particularly touchdown passes to Hasty and Ferguson—separated a close game that could have swung on a single sequence.
turningPoint: Ferguson's 1-yard touchdown run late in the third quarter gave LSU a two-score lead at a moment when Michigan was gaining momentum behind Wendtland's ground game, forcing the Wolverines into a pass-heavy final quarter that played to LSU's defensive strengths.

unsungHero.name: Mangum
unsungHero.why: His seven tackles and relentless effort in coverage allowed LSU's secondary to operate safely while limiting Michigan's ability to establish rhythm offensively, yet the stat sheet doesn't capture how many times he forced early incompletions or kills plays before they developed.
